pullRequest.title: Add Trackio integration for experiment tracking
pullRequest.body:
Adds logging support with [Trackio](https://github.com/gradio-app/trackio). Since Trackio runs locally, not api token needs to be specified. Logging with trackio happens if `trackio` is installed as a package. Summary of changes:

- Adds Trackio integration for experiment tracking and logging.
- Implements `TrackioLogger` class following the same pattern as the Neptune integration from PR #13
- Enables simultaneous logging to multiple platforms (W&B, Neptune, and Trackio)

Closes: #6

pullRequest.title: fix: correct margin calculation in DPO training
pullRequest.body:
fix: correct margin calculation in DPO training

Remove redundant dpo_beta multiplication in margin computation.
The chosen_rewards and rejected_rewards already include the dpo_beta
factor, so margin should not multiply by dpo_beta again.

This fix only affects logging metrics and does not impact training.

Fixes #21

pullRequest.title: fix(wandb): recursively serialize nested objects in dump_config
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #63

This is an alternative solution to #168 that addresses the root cause rather than suppressing the error.

## The Problem

When using `@chz.chz` decorated classes that contain non-serializable objects (like `vf_env` in `VerifiersRLDatasetBuilder`), wandb throws a `ConfigError` on resume:

```
ConfigError: Attempted to change value of key "vf_env" from 
<verifiers.envs.singleturn_env.SingleTurnEnv object at 0x7fd46752da30> to 
<verifiers.envs.singleturn_env.SingleTurnEnv object at 0x7fd46752db40>
```

This happens because `dump_config` calls `chz.asdict()` which returns the raw object reference. When resuming, the same object type gets a different memory address, and wandb sees this as a changed value.

## Why This Fix is Better

PR #168 proposes `allow_val_change=True` which suppresses the error but doesn't fix the underlying problem - the config still logs useless memory addresses like `<SingleTurnEnv object at 0x...>`.

This PR fixes the root cause by making `dump_config` recursively serialize nested objects. Now `vf_env` logs as:
```python
{'max_turns': 5, 'reward_scale': 1.0}
```

Instead of:
```python
<SingleTurnEnv object at 0x7fd46752da30>
```

## The Fix

Two lines changed - recursively apply `dump_config` to values from `chz.asdict()` and `asdict()`:

```python
# Before
elif chz.is_chz(config):
    return chz.asdict(config)

# After  
elif chz.is_chz(config):
    return {k: dump_config(v) for k, v in chz.asdict(config).items()}
```

## Testing

I reproduced the exact error and validated the fix:

```python
# Reproduction
builder1 = VerifiersRLDatasetBuilder(vf_env=SingleTurnEnv())
builder2 = VerifiersRLDatasetBuilder(vf_env=SingleTurnEnv())

config1 = dump_config(builder1)  # Different memory addresses
config2 = dump_config(builder2)

wandb.config.update(config1)
wandb.config.update(config2)  # ConfigError before fix, works after
```

Also verified that existing functionality still works:
- Simple chz classes
- Nested chz classes  
- Regular dataclasses
- Dicts, lists, enums

pullRequest.title: Fix constant group removal
pullRequest.body:
Constant group removal is currently broken. `remove_constant_reward_groups` will always return at least one trajectory group (even if all should be removed). However, `do_group_rollout_and_filter_constant_reward` calls it with only one trajectory group so nothing will ever get removed! This PR switches sync implementation to use `remove_constant_reward_groups` on the full set of trajectory groups. The streaming sync implementation just needed a guard on all groups being None. The async implementation already supported a trajectory group being None, so nothing was done.

Closes #155

pullRequest.title: fix: fix grammar in Search-R1 README
pullRequest.body:
Change "also releases" to "also released" to use correct past tense
when describing already published research paper and data.

Fixes  #157

pullRequest.title: fix: correct pig latin training examples for proper consonant cluster…
pullRequest.body:
fix: #165 
correct pig latin training examples for proper consonant cluster handling

- Fix "split" → "it-splay" (move entire "spl" consonant cluster)
- Fix "quantum" → "antum-quay" (treat "qu" as single phonetic unit)
- Resolves inconsistent phonological rules in training data
- Addresses Issue #165: flawed tinker code for pig latin

This ensures the model learns consistent linguistic patterns instead of
contradictory character-level rules that lead to training confusion.

pullRequest.title: fix: normalize SFT weights per-example for consistent gradient magnitudes
pullRequest.body:
## Summary
- Normalize per-token weights in `datum_from_model_input_weights` to sum to 1.0 per example
- Produces a token-mean loss instead of a token-sum loss, making gradient magnitudes consistent across batches with variable-length sequences
- Adds `normalize_weights` parameter (default `True`) to opt out if needed via `normalize_weights=False`

## Motivation

The current token-sum loss causes batches with more target tokens to produce proportionally larger gradients. With variable-length sequences (common in chat/instruction datasets), this effectively makes the learning rate fluctuate with per-batch token count. As @joschu noted, this is mostly masked by Adam but can cause instability with high-variance sequence length datasets.

## Breaking change note

This PR defaults `normalize_weights=True`, which silently changes loss behavior for all existing callers of `datum_from_model_input_weights` (SFT, preference, DPO, VLM classifier). The old token-sum behavior is recoverable by passing `normalize_weights=False`. This aligns with the direction discussed in #271, but if a more gradual rollout is preferred — e.g., threading the option through `ChatDatasetBuilderCommonConfig` so users can toggle it per-dataset, or defaulting to `False` for backward compatibility — happy to adjust.

## Test plan
- [x] Verify weights sum to 1.0 per example after normalization
- [x] Verify `normalize_weights=False` recovers old behavior
- [x] Verify all-zero weights are handled safely (no division by zero)
- [x] Verify single non-zero weight edge case
- [x] Verify target tokens are correctly left-shifted
- [x] Verify max_length truncation interacts correctly with normalization
- [ ] Verify existing recipe smoke tests pass

Fixes #271

pullRequest.title: fix: populate metrics from OptimStepResponse
pullRequest.body:
fix(supervised): populate metrics from OptimStepResponse

Previously, the result of `optim_step_future` was awaited but ignored in `finish_batch`, causing optimization metrics (like gradient norms) to be dropped.

This commit modifies `tinker_cookbook/supervised/train.py` to:
- Capture the `OptimStepResponse` result from the future.
- Merge its `metrics` field (if present) into the current step's metrics dictionary.

Fixes #275

pullRequest.body:
Closes #283. It includes a new renderer test for all thinking models that checks correspondence `build_supervised_example` and `parse_response` though a round-trip like

```py
continuation_tokens = .render_message([expected_message])[prefill_len:]
.build_supervised_example([user_message, .parse_response(continuation_tokens)]) \
   == .build_generation_prompt([user_message]) + continuation_tokens
```

test plan:

pullRequest.title: Add rolling checkpoints for cheaper training resume
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Adds rolling checkpoints — cheap, frequent resume points that fill the gap when `save_every` is set high. Unlike periodic checkpoints which save both training state and sampler weights, rolling checkpoints only save training state (skipping the sampler-weight export), making them faster and cheaper. After each successful save the previous rolling checkpoint is deleted, bounding storage to ~1 extra checkpoint at any time.

- Add `RollingCheckpointManager` in `checkpoint_utils.py` with async (SL/RL) and sync (DPO) interfaces
- Integrate into all three training loops with two new config fields: `rolling_save_every` and `rolling_ttl_seconds`
- Pass through rolling config in `chat_sl` recipe CLI for smoke testing
- Add unit tests (cadence logic, save/delete lifecycle, error handling) and recipe-level smoke test (train + resume)
- Add documentation in `docs/save-load.mdx`

Closes #313

## Design

### Two independent checkpoint streams

Periodic (`save_every`) and rolling (`rolling_save_every`) checkpoints are fully independent:

| | Periodic | Rolling |
|---|---|---|
| **Saves** | Training state + sampler weights | Training state only (no sampler export) |
| **TTL** | 7 days (default) | 2 hours (default) |
| **Retention** | Kept for evaluation and export | Auto-deleted after next rolling save succeeds |
| **Use case** | Evaluation, weight export, long-term resume | Cheap, frequent resume points |

When both fire on the same step, the rolling save is skipped (the periodic checkpoint is strictly more valuable).

### Non-blocking async saves

In async training loops (SL, RL), rolling saves run as background `asyncio.Task`s that overlap with the next training step. The save only blocks if the previous one hasn't finished by the next step (unlikely for state-only saves). DPO uses a synchronous path since its training loop is sync.

### Crash safety

`finalize()` is called **after** the final checkpoint save in all three loops, ensuring the last entry in `checkpoints.jsonl` always points to valid server-side data. In every crash scenario, at least one valid checkpoint exists:

- Crash before new save fires → old rolling checkpoint still valid
- Crash during save → old checkpoint not yet deleted
- Crash after save, before delete → both exist; resume from newer; TTL cleans old
- Crash after periodic save → resume from periodic; rolling cleaned by TTL

### Backward compatible

`rolling_save_every=0` (default) disables the feature entirely. Rolling checkpoints use the same `checkpoints.jsonl` format and `get_last_checkpoint()` picks them up automatically.

## Configuration

```python
config = sl_train.Config(
    save_every=20,              # periodic checkpoints (state + sampler)
    rolling_save_every=1,       # rolling checkpoints every step (resume-only)
    rolling_ttl_seconds=7200,   # 2-hour safety net TTL (default)
)
```

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests for `_should_save` logic (cadence, step 0, periodic collision, disabled)
- [x] Async integration tests with mocked Tinker client (save/delete lifecycle, finalize cleanup)
- [x] Error handling tests (save failure swallowed, delete failure swallowed)
- [x] Recipe smoke test: train 4 steps with `rolling_save_every=1`, verify `checkpoints.jsonl` contents, resume to step 6

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

pullRequest.title: Fix final checkpoint batch field that breaks resume
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Closes #367.

### The bug

The final checkpoint in multi-epoch trainers (SL, DPO) writes `batch=n_batches` to `checkpoints.jsonl`. For example, a 2-epoch run with 9 batches/epoch produces:

```
 name      epoch  batch
 000003    0      3
 000006    0      6
 000009    1      0
 000012    1      3
 000015    1      6
 final     2      9     ← batch=9 is out of range (valid: 0-8)
```

On resume, <https://github.com/thinking-machines-lab/tinker-cookbook/blob/4fe0d63/tinker_cookbook/supervised/train.py#L171-L172|start_batch is read from the checkpoint> and used in the <https://github.com/thinking-machines-lab/tinker-cookbook/blob/4fe0d63/tinker_cookbook/supervised/train.py#L362-L367|training loop>:

```python
for epoch_idx in range(start_epoch, config.num_epochs):
    start_batch_idx = start_batch if epoch_idx == start_epoch else 0
    for batch_idx in range(start_batch_idx, n_batches):
```

With `start_batch=9` and `n_batches=9`, the inner loop is `range(9, 9)` — empty. The entire epoch is silently skipped. If the user works around this by adding more epochs, the step counter is inflated (`step = epoch * n_batches + batch`), causing the LR scheduler to produce an erroneously low learning rate.

### The fix

Write `batch=0` instead of `batch=n_batches`, meaning "resume at the start of the next epoch." With `start_batch=0`, the inner loop becomes `range(0, 9)` — all 9 batches train correctly.

### Also fixes

- **DPO final checkpoint TTL:** was `config.ttl_seconds` (7 days default), now `None` (kept indefinitely) to match all other final checkpoints
- **Distillation final checkpoint:** add explicit `ttl_seconds=None` (was relying on default)

### Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `supervised/train.py` | `batch=n_batches` → `batch=0` |
| `preference/train_dpo.py` | `batch=n_batches` → `batch=0`, `ttl_seconds=None` |
| `distillation/train_on_policy.py` | Explicit `ttl_seconds=None` |

## Test plan

- [x] `uv run pytest tinker_cookbook/` — 368 passed, 37 skipped
- [x] `uv run pyright tinker_cookbook/` — 0 errors
- [x] `uv run pre-commit run --all-files` — all passed

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

pullRequest.title: Fix Kimi-K2-Thinking tokenizer failing with newer transformers
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Fixes #378.

- The pinned HuggingFace revision (`612681931a`) for `moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Thinking` used a custom `tokenization_kimi.py` that imported `bytes_to_unicode` from `transformers.models.gpt2.tokenization_gpt2`. This import path was removed in newer versions of the `transformers` library, causing an `ImportError`.
- Moonshot fixed this upstream by moving the import to `transformers.convert_slow_tokenizer` (which works in both old and new `transformers` versions).
- This PR updates the pinned revision from the older `612681931a8c906ddb349f8ad0f582cb552189cd` to the latest `a51ccc050d73dab088bf7b0e2dd9b30ae85a4e55`, which includes this fix. The tokenizer vocabulary and behavior are unchanged.

## Test plan

- [x] Verified tokenizer loads correctly with the new revision (`vocab_size=163840`, encode/decode round-trips)
- [x] `pytest tinker_cookbook/tests/test_renderers.py` — 139 passed

pullRequest.title: Fix gate_up_proj interleave index always 0 in merge script
pullRequest.body:
## Summary
- Fix bug where `idx` for selecting gate vs up slots in fused `gate_up_proj` was always 0, causing `up_proj` LoRA deltas to overwrite gate slots instead of up slots
- Affects **6 models** across 3 architecture families: GPT-OSS (interleaved layout), Qwen3.5 MoE, and Qwen3-VL MoE (concatenated layout)
- Determine `idx` from original key name before renaming to `.gate_up_proj`

Closes #457

## Affected models

| Model | Layout | How broken |
|---|---|---|
| gpt-oss-20b, gpt-oss-120b | Interleaved `[g0,u0,g1,u1,...]` | up delta → even indices (gate slots) instead of odd (up slots) |
| Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, Qwen3.5-397B-A17B | Concatenated `[gate \| up]` | up delta → first half (gate) instead of second half (up) |
| Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B, Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B | Concatenated `[gate \| up]` | Same as Qwen3.5 |

Not affected: Qwen3 MoE (separate per-expert weights), DeepSeek-V3.1, Kimi-K2/K2.5, all Llama, all dense Qwen — these never enter the fused experts code path. See full impact analysis in PR comments.

## Test plan
- [x] E2E test with tiny GPT-OSS model (from_config, save/load roundtrip) confirms gate/up deltas land in correct interleaved slots (3/3 pass)
- [x] Verified e2e tests fail on original buggy code (2/3 fail) and pass with fix (3/3 pass)
- [x] Existing 369 unit tests pass with no regressions

**Note:** Unit tests and e2e tests for the merge script are not included in this PR since `tinker_cookbook/scripts/` is currently excluded from pytest discovery and has no test infrastructure. These will be added in a follow-up that integrates the merge logic into the cookbook module with proper test coverage.

## Follow-up
- Integrate the merge script into the cookbook module with unit tests and smoke tests to guardrail weight merging going forward

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

pullRequest.title: Fix build_lora_adapter for Nemotron empty expert LoRA and fused Mamba projections
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Fixes #547. `build_lora_adapter` crashed on Nemotron-3 models with `WeightsAdapterError: Expert LoRA weights must be 3D`.

### Background: Nemotron-3 architecture

Nemotron-3 (Nano-30B, Super-120B) is a hybrid architecture with three layer types:
- **Mamba layers** (~40-50% of layers) — selective state space model
- **MoE layers** (~40-50%) — mixture of experts with 128 (Nano) or 512 (Super) routed experts + 1 shared expert
- **Attention layers** (~10%) — standard dense attention

This creates three adapter conversion challenges that standard models (Qwen3, Llama) don't have.

### Issue 1: Empty expert LoRA tensors crash the converter

Nemotron MoE experts have only 2 projections (`up_proj`, `down_proj`) — no `gate_proj`. Tinker internally uses `w1/w2/w3` naming and produces empty `w3` placeholder tensors (`torch.Size([0])`) for the missing projection. Both `_expand_expert_weights` (adapter path) and `plan_expert_ops` (merge path) required 3D tensors and crashed on these empty 1D placeholders.

**Fix:** Skip empty expert LoRA tensors (`numel() == 0`) in both paths.

### Issue 2: Wrong expert key mapping for Nemotron

The hardcoded `_EXPERT_KEY_REMAPS` mapped `w1→gate_proj`, which is correct for standard 3-projection MoE models (Qwen3, DeepSeek) but wrong for Nemotron where `w1` is `up_proj`. Confirmed by inspecting real adapter weights from Tinker:

```
w1 lora_A: (1, 32, 2688)  lora_B: (128, 1856, 32)  → up_proj (hidden→intermediate)
w2 lora_A: (128, 32, 1856) lora_B: (1, 2688, 32)   → down_proj (intermediate→hidden)
w3: empty                                            → no gate_proj in Nemotron
```

**Fix:** Added `expert_key_remaps` field to `MergeProfile` (default: standard 3-projection mapping). Created `_merge_nemotron.py` detector that sets Nemotron-specific remaps (`w1→up_proj`, `w2→down_proj`). Also simplified `plan_expert_ops` from 9 positional args to 8 by passing `profile` directly (matching `plan_standard_op`'s pattern).

### Issue 3: Mamba LoRA uses projections that don't exist in HF/vLLM

Tinker trains `gate_proj` and `x_proj` as separate LoRA on Mamba layers, but both the HF checkpoint and vLLM fuse these into a single `in_proj` module (`nn.Linear` in HF, `MergedColumnParallelLinear` in vLLM). Neither PEFT nor vLLM can load adapter keys that reference modules that don't exist in the model.

The `in_proj` layout in the HF checkpoint is:
```
in_proj output = [gate/z (intermediate) | x (intermediate) | B | C | dt]
                  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
                  gate_proj LoRA here     x_proj LoRA here     (rest: no LoRA)
```

**Fix:** Added `fused_projection_map` to `MergeProfile` for Nemotron: `("in_proj", ("gate_proj", "x_proj"))`. During adapter conversion, component LoRA weights are merged into a single `in_proj` LoRA using block-diagonal construction:

- `lora_A`: concatenate component A matrices → rank doubles to `2r`
- `lora_B`: place each component B in the correct row slice of the fused output, zeros elsewhere

The PEFT config uses `rank_pattern` and `alpha_pattern` to keep the LoRA scaling factor (`alpha/rank`) correct for the doubled-rank module.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `_merge.py` | Add `expert_key_remaps`, `fused_projection_map` fields to `MergeProfile`; register Nemotron detector |
| `_merge_nemotron.py` | **New.** Detect `NemotronHForCausalLM`, set Nemotron-specific expert remaps and fused projection map |
| `_adapter.py` | Skip empty experts; use profile-driven expert remaps; merge fused Mamba projections into `in_proj`; support `rank_pattern`/`alpha_pattern` in PEFT config |
| `_merge_utils.py` | `plan_expert_ops`: skip empty experts, take `profile` instead of `is_fused`/`is_interleaved`/`expert_key_remaps` |
| `_merge_default.py`, `_merge_deepseek.py`, `_merge_gpt_oss.py`, `_merge_qwen3_5.py` | Pass `profile` to `plan_expert_ops` |
| `adapter_test.py` | 6 new tests: empty expert skip, expert key mapping, in_proj merge (block-diagonal structure + rank/alpha patterns), 2D check, shared experts |
| `merge_test.py` | 4 new tests: Nemotron profile detection, empty expert merge |
| `test_nemotron.py` | `test_moe_expert_adapter` on both Nano and Super: synthetic adapter with all 3 layer types (Mamba + MoE + Attention), verifies conversion + vLLM serving |

## Test plan

- [x] 96 unit tests pass (`pytest tinker_cookbook/weights/adapter_test.py tinker_cookbook/weights/merge_test.py`)
- [x] Real Tinker adapter conversion verified for both Nano (12K PEFT keys) and Super (82K PEFT keys)
- [x] vLLM e2e serving verified with real adapters on Nemotron-3-Nano (TP=2) and Nemotron-3-Super (TP=4)
- [x] Sensible generation output confirmed with multiple prompts on both models

pullRequest.title: Add MXFP4 shard merge support for GPT-OSS models
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

GPT-OSS checkpoints use MX block-quantized FP4 weights (`_blocks` + `_scales` + `_bias`). This PR adds `MXBlockShardHooks` that dequantizes MXFP4 to BF16, applies LoRA merge, and re-quantizes back — preserving the on-disk format.

Detection is config-driven (`quant_method in {mxfp4, mxfp8}`), not model-family-specific. Any future model shipping MXFP4/MXFP8 gets these hooks automatically. GPU acceleration supported via `device` parameter plumbed through `build_sharded`.

Closes #587.

### New files
- `_mxfp4.py` — Pure PyTorch FP4 E2M1 pack/unpack/quantize/dequantize (no external deps)
- `_export/_shard_mx_block.py` — `MXBlockShardHooks` implementing `ShardHooks` protocol

### Modified
- `_export/_shard.py` — MXFP4 detection + device parameter forwarding
- `_export/__init__.py` — device parameter plumbed to standard merge path

### Verified
- 258 unit tests pass (13 new MXFP4 math tests)
- E2e with real `openai/gpt-oss-20b` adapter (GPU): merge succeeds, 459 keys, blocks format preserved
- GPT-OSS 120B: testing on CPU (GPU OOM on single H200 due to 64-expert fused tensor size — per-expert GPU strategy is a follow-up)

## Test plan
- [x] MXFP4 math: pack/unpack roundtrip, exact FP4 values, E8M0 scale encoding
- [x] Quantize/dequantize roundtrip within tolerance
- [x] Full unit test suite (258 passed)
- [x] ruff, pyright clean
- [x] E2e merge with real GPT-OSS-20B adapter (GPU)
- [x] E2e merge with GPT-OSS-120B (CPU, running)

pullRequest.title: Fix FP8 expert quantization for fused 3D expert tensors
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

- Fix `post_merge_transform` to handle fused 3D expert tensors `[num_experts, out, in]` by quantizing each expert slice independently
- Previously, only 2D per-expert keys ending in `.weight` were quantized — Qwen3-VL's fused format (`experts.gate_up_proj`, `experts.down_proj`) was silently skipped
- Also fixes `_weight_scale_key` and `verify_fp8_output` to handle keys without `.weight` suffix

Closes #596

## Models affected

- **Qwen3-VL** (only fused 3D expert keys) — now correctly quantized to FP8
- **DeepSeek** (per-expert 2D keys) — unchanged, locked in by existing + new unit tests
- **Qwen3.5** (mixed: per-expert 2D + fused 3D) — per-expert keys still quantized, fused keys now also quantized

## Test plan

- [x] All 52 unit tests pass (`quantized_test.py`) including 14 new tests
- [x] Full unit test suite passes (1120 passed)
- [x] e2e: `test_qwen3_vl.py::TestQuantized::test_fp8_experts` — PASSED (4m22s)

pullRequest.title: Fix eval resume: restore metrics and error counts from JSONL
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Fix a bug where resuming a benchmark run (restarting with the same `save_dir`) produced incorrect `num_truncated` and `num_errors` counts.

## Problem

`_load_completed()` only restored `idx → reward` from `trajectories.jsonl`. Per-example `metrics` and `error` status were discarded. This caused:
- `num_truncated = 0` for resumed examples (truncation is counted from `max_tokens_reached`/`context_overflow` metrics)
- `num_errors` undercounted (errors from prior run not restored)
- `score_completed` incorrect after resume

## Fix

`_load_completed()` now returns `_ResumedExample(reward, metrics, is_error)`. The resume logic pre-fills rewards, metrics, and error counts into the runner state, so aggregation is correct regardless of whether examples ran in this process or were restored from disk.

## Test plan

- [x] 70 unit tests pass
- [x] Ruff clean

Fixes #607.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

pullRequest.title: Fix example_id on truncated trajectories + use example_id-based resume
pullRequest.body:
## Summary

Two related fixes for eval trajectory storage and resumability.

## Problem 1: Truncated trajectories have wrong example_id

When a thinking model hits `max_tokens`, `EnvFromMessageEnv.step()` returns early — `MessageEnv.step()` never runs, so `example_id` is never set in transition logs. The trajectory falls back to `str(idx)` which is positional and unstable.

In an AIME pass@4 run, 9 of 96 trajectories had numeric IDs (`"13"`, `"14"`) instead of hash-based IDs (`"aime_2026_72013a3d92a7"`). All 9 were `max_tokens_reached` truncations.

**Fix:** `EnvFromMessageEnv` now forwards `example_id` from the inner `MessageEnv` as an instance attribute. `_trajectory_to_stored` receives it as a required parameter.

## Problem 2: Resume matches by positional idx (fragile)

`_load_completed` keyed by `idx` (position in env list). If `max_examples` changes or the dataset order differs between runs, `idx=5` in run A could be a different problem than `idx=5` in run B.

**Fix:** Resume now matches by `example_id` (content hash from `make_example_id`). This is robust to shuffling and subset changes.

## Changes

- **`EnvFromMessageEnv`** (`message_env.py`): Adds `self.example_id: str` forwarded from inner `MessageEnv`
- **`_get_example_id(env, idx)`**: New helper replacing 3 inline `getattr` patterns
- **`_load_completed`**: Keys by `example_id` instead of `idx`
- **Resume pre-fill**: Matches envs by `example_id` via `env_example_ids` list
- **`_trajectory_to_stored`**: `example_id` is now a required parameter (no internal fallback)
- **Warning**: Logs once per benchmark if envs lack `example_id`

## Backward compatibility

The change to `EnvFromMessageEnv` is safe:

- **Additive only** — adds one `str` instance attribute; no API changes, no removed methods
- **No existing code accesses `env.example_id`** — the RL training path (`rollouts.py`, `rollout_strategy.py`) never touches it
- **No subclasses** of `EnvFromMessageEnv` in the codebase
- **Picklable** — verified full pickle round-trip with a real renderer; `example_id` is a plain `str`, adds negligible size
- **All 26 existing `message_env_test.py` tests pass** — test MessageEnvs don't set `example_id`, so `self.example_id = ""`, which is harmless
- **`getattr(message_env, "example_id", "")`** is safe for any MessageEnv including those without `example_id`

## Test plan

- [x] 70 eval benchmark tests pass
- [x] 26 message_env tests pass
- [x] Ruff clean
- [x] Pickle round-trip verified with real EnvFromMessageEnv

Fixes #608 (partial — enables example_id-based resume, pass@k resumability is a separate issue).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
